issue_title: Loading from list of filenames should return a list
issue_body:
Related to [this section](https://github.com/uiri/toml/blob/master/toml.py#L41-L48) of code.
When calling `.loads([])` my expectation would be to get a list, not a dictionary with all the values merged together. I do not really see a use case for the latter. It also causes unexpected rewrites. 

Example:
```Python
with open('file1.toml', 'w') as f:
    f.write('a = 1\n')

with open('file2.toml', 'w') as f:
    f.write('a = 2\n')

tom = toml.load(['file1.toml', 'file2.toml']);
print(tom) # Prints {'a': 2} 
```

Anyways.  It also appears in some prior versions.

issue_title: Sending dialog: Replace play button while sending with pause button (...and add pause feature)
Ability to clear recording buffer whilst recording
issue_body:
This is more a feature request rather than a bug.

Would it be possible to clear the recording buffer whilst samples are being captured?
Reason being is sometimes i'm not sure when a signal will be present and the recording gets quite large, it would be good to be able to press clear whilst its recording to start fresh if you know what i mean.

Pressing stop and clear and start takes too long for the SDR (lime in my case) to start recording so there is a chance signal could be missed.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/11001973/43117783-fb2c70e2-8f51-11e8-99ce-9495e5323df8.png)

issue_title: Losing data in Analysis tab
issue_body: Eg. In the analysis tab i usually change to hex view so i see the data a little easier and i hide some rows that are not of interest, what i found though if i click a different tab say the generator tab and go back to the analysis tab all the data seems to vanish.

issue_title: HTTPError not caught as expected in flower.views.auth.LoginHandler
issue_body:
This is a small but annoying bug that occurs in the `_on_auth()` method on `flower.views.auth.LoginHandler` (related to PR #359).  When `tornado.web.HTTPError` is raised, the expected behaviour is that the error is caught and handled by displaying the error message to the user using a template file. Instead, the exception is logged to stderr, and nothing is displayed to the user, causing the request to hang.

I suspect this is because `_on_auth()` is executed as a callback in `get_authenticated_user()` (which is itself defined as an instance method on `tornado.auth.GoogleOAuth2Mixin`), which means it's being converted into a future by the `tornado.auth._auth_return_future` decorator in a way that causes exceptions to be handled differently. I'd love to hear any thoughts on what's happening under the hood and if there's a way to force HTTPError to be handled as expected, as I can't find a good fix for this.

issue_title: vint poorly handle functions and their scopes
issue_body:
Hello.

Consider the following VimL code:
```
call vundle#begin('~/.vim/plugins/')
Plugin 'todesking/vint-syntastic'
Plugin 'scrooloose/syntastic'
call vundle#end()
```

vint complains:
```
Make the scope explicit like `g:vundle#begin` (see Anti-pattern of vimrc (Scope of variable))
Make the scope explicit like `g:vundle#end` (see Anti-pattern of vimrc (Scope of variable))
```
The comment about `Scope of variable` is out of place here. Anti-pattern of Vim also says nothing about function scopes.

Now consider the following code that describes a function that should be available globally:
```
function! mysuperplugin#do()
    echom 'Hello world!'
endfunction
```
Again vint complains:
```
Make the scope explicit like `g:mysuperplugin#do` (see Anti-pattern of vimrc (Scope of variable))
```

Logically this function should have `g:` scope, but Vim actually forbids it:
```
The name must be made of alphanumeric characters and '_',
and must start with a capital or "s:" (see above).
Note that using "b:" or "g:" is not allowed.
```
See `:h E884` for more info.

While the previous behavior is still somewhat tolerable, the following code:
```
function! g:mysuperplugin#do()
    echom 'Hello world!'
endfunction
```
is valid for vint, while for Vim it is clearly not. This is really bad.

Please fix.

issue_title: problems with: user defined unit-systems
issue_body:
Hi guys,

currently I'm working with pint 0.7.2 and try out the new Unit System functionality. I want to perform a couple of unit-system transformations and run into some issues. From the [latest pint doc](http://pint.readthedocs.org/en/latest/systems.html) I saw the following example:

```python
import pint
print('pint version: {0}'.format(pint.__version__))

ureg = pint.UnitRegistry(system='mks')
q = 3600. * ureg.meter / ureg.hour
print('default_system: {0}'.format(ureg.default_system))
print('q: {0}'.format(q.to_base_units()))

ureg.default_system = 'cgs'
print('default_system: {0}'.format(ureg.default_system))
print('q: {0}'.format(q.to_base_units()))
```

which runs fine. Now I wanted to define some user specific default systems. One way to work with user defined default systems is to save the definition in a extra file and load them with `ureg.load_definitions('/your/path/to/my_def.txt') `. 

But there seem to be a another way to generate systems directly in the code, with out saving the definition to a separate file. In the testsuit `test_systems.py` I found the following way to define a new system:
```python
lines = ['@system mmkgms using international',
                  'millimeter',
                  'kilogram',
                  'millisecond',
                  ]

s = ureg.System.from_lines(lines, lambda x: x)

ureg.default_system = 'mmkgms'
print('default_system: {0}'.format(ureg.default_system))
print('q: {0}'.format(q.to_base_units()))

```
But this version dose not work as I expect it to work. I expect a `1.0 millimeter / millisecond` and got a `1.0 meter / second` .

Can someone help me to solve this problem? Or dose I have to keep the extra file solution?

Thanks
Tilo

issue_title: Dimensional reduction
issue_body:
I was really excited to see how pint handled a lot of "gotcha" issues, until I ran into something that seemed super simple that pint throws a curve on.

Maybe there's something obvious I'm missing, but it seems like pint should be able to recognize when you have a ratio of two quantities with the same dimensionality and automatically do the conversions to reduce them.  For example: 
    In [1]: import pint
    In [2]: ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()
    In [3]: density = 3*ureg.g / ureg.L
    In [4]: volume = 23*ureg.milliliter
    In [5]: mass = density * volume
    In [6]: print(mass)
    69.0 gram * milliliter / liter
    In [7]: mass.dimensionality
    Out[7]: <UnitsContainer({'[mass]': 1.0})>

So pint even correctly recognizes the dimensionality, and is just carrying around this constant "milliliter/liter" factor.  Users shouldn't have to take an extra step to do something as simple as multiply density*volume to get mass or speed*time to get distance

issue_title: Differences between Registry.parse_expression() and Quantity()
issue_body:
I find it strange that Registry.parse_expression(string) and Quantity(string) return different values for some input strings, especially since they are described as interchangeable in the docs. 

For example, parse_expression("1") returns int(1), whereas Quantity("1") returns a dimensionless quantity with a value of 1, as desired.

Also, it would be nice if parse_expression could handle ints in addition to strings like Quantity() does.

issue_title: Support for the math module
issue_body:
Since Pint support things like `numpy.sqrt(…)`, it would be nice if it allowed people to use the math module as well (for instance through a `pint.pintmath` module).

One consequence of this is that people try the following, but it fails:
```
from uncertainties import umath

umath.sqrt(<pint number with units>)
```

Removing this limitation would be nice.

I guess that using new `pintmath` module instead would be the simplest thing to do?

issue_title: ip6.IP6._protosw.update() is premature
issue_body:
_From [liulk@google.com](https://code.google.com/u/liulk@google.com/) on November 28, 2012 12:43:09_

Using Python 2.7 which randomizes hash key order, ip.__load_protos() may implicitly __import__() the ip6 module before some of the other protocols are populated into ip.IP._protosw.  If we do IP6._protosw.update(ip.IP._protosw) as is done currently, then IP6 would only get a subset of protocols that happen to be defined so far, and due to hash key randomization, this could very well be the empty set.

Moreover, ip6 could not import ip before class IP6 is defined.  The workaround here is to do this after class IP6, forcing the sharing of dictionary rather than making a copy.

import ip
IP6._protosw = ip.IP._protosw

Attaching a patch which I verified to work.

liulk

**Attachment:** [ip6.py.diff](http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=99)

_Original issue: http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=99_

issue_title: Building libraries with multiple source files
issue_body:
Hey Stefano,

I think there is a bug when building a library that consists of more than one source file.  If the library is only one file, then it works, but if there are multiple files then it doesn't.  It seems like the object code for anything other than the main file is not getting archived in the library.  

Example (for my [fortran-astrodynamics-toolkit](https://github.com/jacobwilliams/Fortran-Astrodynamics-Toolkit) project): 
```Fortran
FoBiS.py build -compiler gnu -colors -cflags "-c -O2" -dbld ./lib/ -s ./src/ -dmod ./ -dobj ./ -t fortran_astrodynamics_toolkit.f90 -o libfat.a -mklib static
```
All the modules are compiled, but the resulting ```.a``` file doesn't contain any of the code from any module other than the main one.  What should happen is that all the .o files that were compiled should be added to the library (using something like ```ar -rcs libfat.a ./lib/*.o```).

issue_title: Slow retrieval of point data
issue_body:
Hi
I use pygrib to plot charts from GFS grib2 files (a UK subset). This works great and quick enough for my purposes.  I am now looking into plotting time series of point data from the same grib files, but am finding that the process of extracting the data values for a given point to be far to slow.

I was hoping to create time series plots of variables such as temperature and winds on the fly - but on my system it takes over 30 seconds to get and process the data for one grid point from the first timestep to the 81st (10 days).

I have tried looping through individual grib files for each time step, extracting the values for the desired grid point and storing in a list. I also tried concatenating the grib files into one master grb2 file that contains all time steps, then looping through the messages for a given variable, but this was much slower.

I am used to using GrADS where a full gribfile for all timesteps could be opened, the desired timesteps selected (set t 1 81) and then a plot produced for these times for a given variable.

Is there a way to specify the lat/lon subset required at the open/index stage, or can this only be done to  individual grib messages eg grb.data(lat1=lat,lat2=lat,lon1=lon,lon2=lon)? I searched through all the docs but can't see that you deal with time sequences for point data anywhere. I'd be grateful for any pointers you can provide.
cheers
Dan

issue_title: Choice as a shorthand case in 1.1
issue_body:
Based on code review, `statements.v_expand_1_children` does not appear to consider `choice` as a shorthand `case` statement and therefore fails to create an artificial `case` for it in 1.1.

```python
    elif stmt.keyword == 'choice':
        shorthands = ['leaf', 'leaf-list', 'container', 'list',
                      'anyxml', 'anydata']
```

```
   From 7.9.2.

   As a shorthand, the "case" statement can be omitted if the branch
   contains a single "anydata", "anyxml", "choice", "container", "leaf",
   "list", or "leaf-list" statement.  In this case, the case node still
   exists in the schema tree, and its identifier is the same as the
   identifier of the child node.
```

issue_title: Question: How scripts work in fusesoc?
issue_body:
Hi,
I tried to add tcl a tcl script to a core to be build with Vivado. Specifically I have a build script for a Vivado IP  integrator block design (created with the write_bd macro) which I want to add to my build.

The idea behind is, that the toplevel of my design is build with IP integrator (basically this is the safest and easiest way to combine custom RTL with Xilinx IP like DDR3 RAM interface).

So my core file looks like this:

````
CAPI=1
[main]
name = ::bonfire-arty:0
depend = ::bonfire-axi:0 ::bonfire-axi4l2wb:0 ::zpuino-uart:0
backend = vivado


[fileset rtl]
files = bonfire_axi_sysio.vhd wb_io.vhd
    ../lib/bonfire-soc/spi/wb_spi_interface.vhd
    ../lib/bonfire-soc/spi/spimaster.vhd
    BootMem.vhd
    wishbone_subsystem.vhd
file_type=vhdlSource

[fileset xdc]
files = Arty.xdc
file_type=xdc
scope=synth

[fileset scripts]
file_type=tclSource
files = ../scripts/fusesoc_build_1.tcl  ../scripts/bd_bonfire_arty_1704.tcl  ../scripts/fusesoc_build_2.tcl


[simulator]
toplevel = bonfire_axi_top

[ghdl]
analyze_options = --ieee=synopsys
run_options = --ieee=synopsys

[vivado]
part=xc7a35ticsg324-1L
````

When running a fusesoc build the Vivado tcl will source the scripts before adding any files from fileset rtl. But I need it the other way around, I want my scripts be added between project setup and implementation run. 

I tried to add a [script] section, but I doen't change anything. But this section is also not really documented what it exactly means. Currently I can work around, with running build with the --setup option, move the scripts to the right place in the build tcl and then run it. 

From the three scripts in the example above the first one should be run before adding rtl (it sets things like the board type and custom IP repo directories, the second and third should run after rtl (the last script creates the toplevel hd wrapper for the block design and set it as top module.

I'm not sure if my ideas are in scope of FuseSoC, so consider this issue really as question.

issue_title: Powerset generator in EFS consumed 240 GB of RAM for ~ 300 features
issue_body:
The _candidates_ object in _EFS.fit_ implements a standard powerset generator using _itertools_. This function quickly consumed 240 GB of RAM with ~300 features for the range (0, 20). This makes sense given that this generates around 7.5^30 8-byte integers, making it too large for memory even in a cluster environment.

I'm currently testing a solution utilizing _joblib_ _delayed_ and _Parallel_ along with _dask.array_, which looks like this:

```
import dask.array as da

def powerset(iterable, min_features, max_features)
    """
    returns a generator following the form:
    powerset([1,2,3] for range [1,3]) --> (1,) (2,) (3,) (1,2) (1,3) (2,3) (1,2,3)
    """
    s = list(iterable)
    return chain.from_iterable(combinations(s, r) for r in range(min_features, max_features + 1))

min_features, max_features = [1, 20]
ps = powerset(iterable=X, min_features=min_features, max_features)
def ret(x):
    return x
ps = [Parallel(n_jobs=4)(delayed(ret)(i) for i in ps)]
ps = da.from_delayed(ps)
```

So far, memory consumption appears dramatically reduced, but CPU utilization is 100% on a single core.

Cheers,

Adam

issue_title: Flushing the stdout
issue_body:
I have seen when I am printing some messages using print() statement prior to creating object of pyprind.ProgBar, the process bar gets split into separate parts like example below:

```
0%                          100%
Dataset: muct-subjects.txt   Task: genuine  VGG: orig/
Number of Subjects:  276
[##############################] | ETA: 00:00:00
Total time elapsed: 00:00:06

```
In order to fix this, I had to manually flush the stdout with:
```
import sys
sys.stdout.flush()
```
and that will fix it. 

Just as a suggestion, it will be helpful to put the `sys.stdout.flush()` in the constructor to take care of it.

Is it possible to use pysodium with crypto_secretbox_easy? Is it intentionally left out or is this something a pull request would be welcome for? issue_title: Using CLOCK_MONOTONIC rather than in Linux?
issue_body:
Hi!

I noticed that the current code uses CLOCK_MONOTONIC (value 1) rather than CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW (value 4) on Linux
(https://github.com/atdt/monotonic/blob/master/monotonic.py#L101)
and is therefore

> affected by the incremental adjustments performed by adjtime(3) and NTP.

according to `man clock_gettime` on Debian wheezy.

The situation in `/usr/include` is this:
```
$ fgrep -R "define CLOCK_MONOTONIC" /usr/include/ | fgrep -v COARSE
/usr/include/linux/time.h:#define CLOCK_MONOTONIC			1
/usr/include/linux/time.h:#define CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW		4
/usr/include/bits/time.h:#   define CLOCK_MONOTONIC		1
/usr/include/bits/time.h:#   define CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW		4
/usr/include/x86_64-linux-gnu/bits/time.h:#   define CLOCK_MONOTONIC		1
/usr/include/x86_64-linux-gnu/bits/time.h:#   define CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW		4
```

Is this a bug or a feature?

At least in my case of measuring runtimes (and timouts) my current impression is that I would rather use CLOCK_MONOTONIC_RAW, i.e. be not affected by NTP.

What do you think?

Best, Sebastian

issue_title: GCC failure with pip, virtualenv - solved by installing python-devel
issue_body:
**tl;dr - don't forget to install python-devel on a new system when trying to get resampy installed!**

Not a bug, just me being silly. Posting this for posterity, I'm sure someone else will run into this issue at some point. 

Librosa bought me here.[ My issue is pretty close to this issue](https://github.com/bmcfee/resampy/issues/25). 

System: Fedora 25, python 3.5.2, pip 9.0.1, virtualenv 15.1.0
gcc (GCC) 6.2.1 20160916 (Red Hat 6.2.1-2)

`pip install resampy` fails with 

```
creating build/temp.linux-x86_64-3.5/resampy
    gcc -pthread -Wno-unused-result -Wsign-compare -DDYNAMIC_ANNOTATIONS_ENABLED=1 -DNDEBUG -O2 -g -pipe -Wall -Werror=format-security -Wp,-D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 -fexceptions -fstack-protector-strong --param=ssp-buffer-size=4 -grecord-gcc-switches -specs=/usr/lib/rpm/redhat/redhat-hardened-cc1 -m64 -mtune=generic -D_GNU_SOURCE -fPIC -fwrapv -fPIC -I/home/mike/ve/ml/lib/python3.5/site-packages/numpy/core/include -I/usr/include/python3.5m -c resampy/interp.c -o build/temp.linux-x86_64-3.5/resampy/interp.o
    resampy/interp.c:24:20: fatal error: Python.h: No such file or directory
     #include "Python.h"
                        ^
    compilation terminated.
    error: command 'gcc' failed with exit status 1
```

I cloned the git repo and ran `python setup.py install` and got 

```
running build_ext
building 'resampy.interp' extension
gcc -pthread -Wno-unused-result -Wsign-compare -DDYNAMIC_ANNOTATIONS_ENABLED=1 -DNDEBUG -O2 -g -pipe -Wall -Werror=format-security -Wp,-D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 -fexceptions -fstack-protector-strong --param=ssp-buffer-size=4 -grecord-gcc-switches -specs=/usr/lib/rpm/redhat/redhat-hardened-cc1 -m64 -mtune=generic -D_GNU_SOURCE -fPIC -fwrapv -fPIC -I/home/mike/ve/ml/lib/python3.5/site-packages/numpy/core/include -I/usr/include/python3.5m -c resampy/interp.c -o build/temp.linux-x86_64-3.5/resampy/interp.o
gcc: error: resampy/interp.c: No such file or directory
```

`pip install -e resampy` gives the identical error, `pip install .` likewise. 

And then I'm like, wait a minute, did I install python-devel? *installs, tries `pip install resampy`, success* D'oh!

I compiled my **jni** project as the examples and successfully generated libface.so file on centos7, But when I  called it, It threw an exception

Here's the CMakeLists.txt information
> cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 2.8.12)

## What is the issue?  Can you help me , thanks!

issue_title: MMOD loss goes negative
issue_body:
For some reason it happens on one specific dataset that the MMOD loss goes negative. I believe this should not be possible?

It seems to me that the problem has to do with the positive confidence going higher than it should (perhaps because some of the samples are very easy to learn, or something like that).

More specifically, it seems that the loss goes negative because [here](https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/c2a9dee846cad7b20998ee0f3fae79b6bcb67d43/dlib/dnn/loss.h#L765) some of the `out_data` are large – much larger than the `loss_per_missed_target` that [is added](https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/c2a9dee846cad7b20998ee0f3fae79b6bcb67d43/dlib/dnn/loss.h#L751) to the loss initially (fwiw, I'm using the default losses of 1.0 for both missed targets and false alarms).

I have implemented [a quick fix](https://github.com/reunanen/dlib/commit/e33959968152014ac5b0f9db087cd7581b8f5f6d) that seems to help at least in my case: the loss no longer goes negative, and also the end results are more reasonable and more stable.

However, I do not think my fix is overall correct. In particular the conditional gradient update appears inelegant, if not outright wrong. Nevertheless, in my case the change very clearly helps keep the loss and the gradients sane.

issue_title: 'instanceIndex=(0,)' caused exception SmiError as “ValueRangeConstraint(1, 2147483647) failed at: ”0“” at Integer32
issue_body:
I am new for python and SNMP (as well as pysnmp). After two weeks study, I wrote  a piece of python code which try to send a trap message.
The NotificationType was created by code:
 

       notification = NotificationType(ObjectIdentity('MY_MIB_FILE','myAlarmCleared'),
                                        instanceIndex=(0,),  
                                        objects={
                                            ('MY_MIB_FILE', 'myAlarmId'):
                                                Integer32(111),
                                            ('MY_MIB_FILE', 'mySystemDN'):
                                                self.DisplayString(''),  # currently a null string
                                            ('MY_MIB_FILE', 'myAlarmNotificationSource'):
                                                ObjectIdentifier(
                                                    (1, 3, 6, 1, 4)), # some dummy value
                                            ('MY_MIB_FILE', 'myNotiSequenceNum'):
                                                Integer32(222),
                                            ('MY_MIB_FILE', 'myAlarmManagedObjectInstance'):
                                                self.DisplayString('SubNetwork=nw1,ManagedElement=my-1'),
                                            ('MY_MIB_FILE', 'myAlarmType'):
                                                self.AlarmType(3),
                                            ('MY_MIB_FILE', 'myAlarmProbableCause'):
                                                self.ProbableCause(307),
                                            ('MY_MIB_FILE', 'myAlarmSpecificProblem'):
                                                self.DisplayString('test alarm'),
                                            ('MY_MIB_FILE', 'myAlarmPerceivedSeverity'):
                                                self.AlarmSeverity(6),
                                            ('MY_MIB_FILE', 'myAlarmTime'):
                                                self.DateAndTime(OctetString(self.get_current_date_and_time())),
                                            ('MY_MIB_FILE', 'myAlarmAdditionalText'):
                                                self.DisplayString('alarm testing'),
                                            ('MY_MIB_FILE', 'myAlarmName'):
                                                self.DisplayString('system_testing_alarm'),
                                            ('MY_MIB_FILE', 'myAlarmServiceUser'):
                                                self.DisplayString('Dapeng Jiao'),
                                            ('MY_MIB_FILE', 'myAlarmServiceProvider'):
                                                self.DisplayString('Unknown Service Provider'),
                                            ('MY_MIB_FILE', 'myAlarmSecurityAlarmDetector'):
                                                self.DisplayString('Unknown Alarm Detector'),
                                            ('MY_MIB_FILE', 'myAlarmADMC'):
                                                Integer(1)
                                        }
                                        )


when I execute this code, I got following SmiError:

      File "/vagrant_data/snmpAgent/SOURCES/snmp_notifier_v3.py", line 142, in notifier_start
        cbFun=cbfun
      File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pysnmp/hlapi/asyncore/ntforg.py", line 145, in sendNotification
        vbProcessor.makeVarBinds(snmpEngine, varBinds),
      File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pysnmp/hlapi/varbinds.py", line 51, in makeVarBinds
        varBinds.resolveWithMib(mibViewController)
      File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pysnmp/smi/rfc1902.py", line 1143, in resolveWithMib
        objectIdentity = ObjectIdentity(*notificationObject+self.__instanceIndex).resolveWithMib(mibViewController)
      File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pysnmp/smi/rfc1902.py", line 504, in resolveWithMib
        raise SmiError('Instance index %r to OID convertion failure at object %r: %s' % (self.__args[2:], mibNode.getLabel(), sys.exc_info()[1]))
    pysnmp.smi.error.SmiError: Instance index (0,) to OID convertion failure at object 'myAlarmTime': ConstraintsIntersection(ConstraintsIntersection(ConstraintsIntersection(), ValueRangeConstraint(-2147483648, 2147483647)), ValueRangeConstraint(1, 2147483647)) failed at: "ValueRangeConstraint(1, 2147483647) failed at: "0"" at Integer32

It seems pysnmp doesn't like to add suffix .0 for the variable whose syntax is defined as:

    Integer32().subtype(subtypeSpec=ValueRangeConstraint(1, 2147483647))).setMaxAccess("readwrite")

Does it a bug for pysnmp? 
Or I should not add that .0 suffix?(at least after remove that '0,' from instanceIndex the code can be executed and trap message sent successfully. 
But I was told that 
> A scalar variable has single instance and is identified by suffix .0 .
The object identifier (OID)  with .0 suffix indicates a scalar variable (i.e., single instance) (eg, analogous/category  of  a “table” with only one column)
Each of the varbinds in the notification is scalar for this alarm notification.

> Also there is a explanation about Scalar and Tabular Objects: https://www.webnms.com/cagent/help/technology_used/c_snmp_overview.html#scalar&tabular
Scalar and Tabular Objects
 
>A managed object has both a type (defined in ASN.1) and a value. For example, the SNMP system group variable sysLocation ( this variable is defined in RFC1213-MIB ) has the type, DisplayString and may have the value, "WebNMS Velachery". So in our case, we can define noOfPapers or inkLevel of the printer as a scalar object in the MIB.
 
>Managed objects, in SNMP, are of two types : scalar objects and tabular objects.
 
>A managed object that always has a single instance is called a scalar object. Tabular objects, on the other hand, have multiple instances, such as the rows of a table. For example, the MIB II system group has seven "leaf" variables under it, as illustrated in Figure below. Each of these objects is a scalar object. For example, the value of sysUpTime is the duration of time since re-initialization of a system's network management software (SNMP agent), measured in hundredths of a second.
 
>Tables in SNMP are two-dimensional objects defined as an ASN.1 type called SEQUENCE OF, which allows 0 or more members. Each element of the sequence is an entry (row) in the table, which itself is a sequence of scalar-valued objects. SNMP does not allow tables to be nested within tables.
 
>For example, the MIB II at group contains simply one tabular object, the atTable, which contains one row for each of a system's physical interfaces. Each row in the table is an instance of the object atEntry. Each row contains instances of the scalar-valued leaf objects atIfIndex, atPhysAddress, and atNetAddress. The leaf objects are called columnar objects since the instances of each such object constitute one column in the table. Although these objects have scalar-valued instances, they are not scalar objects because they can have multiple instances.

So seems the .0 is anyway needed, right? (Since it should be a object has a single instance.)
Or there are some misunderstanding from us?

Thanks in advance.

Br,
-Dapeng Jiao


The original MIB defination for that variable is:

    myAlarmCleared NOTIFICATION-TYPE
        OBJECTS {
            myAlarmId,
            cesSystemDN,
            myAlarmNotificationSource,
            cesNotiSequenceNum,
            myAlarmManagedObjectInstance,
            myAlarmType,
            myAlarmProbableCause,
            myAlarmSpecificProblem,
            myAlarmPerceivedSeverity,
            myAlarmTime,
            myAlarmAdditionalText,
            myAlarmName,
            myAlarmServiceUser,
            myAlarmServiceProvider,
            myAlarmSecurityAlarmDetector,
            myAlarmADMC
        }
        STATUS	current
        DESCRIPTION
            "This notification is generated when an alarm is cleared."
        ::=  {  myAlarmNotifications  3  }

    myAlarmTime	OBJECT-TYPE
        SYNTAX	DateAndTime
        MAX-ACCESS	read-only
        STATUS	current
        DESCRIPTION
            "This variable is the time of this Alarm object."
        ::=  {  myAlarmEntry  9  }

    myAlarmEntry OBJECT-TYPE
        SYNTAX	myAlarmEntry
        MAX-ACCESS	not-accessible
        STATUS	current
        DESCRIPTION
            "A row containing one alarm."
        INDEX	{ myAlarmId }
        ::= { myAlarmTable 1 }


and converted python format is:

    myAlarmCleared = NotificationType((1, 3, 6, 1, 4, 1, xxxx, x, xx, x, xxx, x, 6, 3)).setObjects(*(
    ("MY-MIB-FILE", "myAlarmTime"), ("MY-MIB-FILE", "myAlarmName"),
    ("MY-MIB-FILE", "myAlarmServiceUser"), ("MY-MIB-FILE", "myAlarmSpecificProblem"),
    ("MY-MIB-FILE", "cesSystemDN"), ("MY-MIB-FILE", "myAlarmProbableCause"),
    ("MY-MIB-FILE", "myAlarmType"), ("MY-MIB-FILE", "myAlarmAdditionalText"),
    ("MY-MIB-FILE", "myAlarmNotificationSource"), ("MY-MIB-FILE", "myAlarmId"),
    ("MY-MIB-FILE", "myAlarmManagedObjectInstance"), ("MY-MIB-FILE", "myAlarmADMC"),
    ("MY-MIB-FILE", "myAlarmSecurityAlarmDetector"),
    ("MY-MIB-FILE", "myAlarmPerceivedSeverity"), ("MY-MIB-FILE", "cesNotiSequenceNum"),
    ("MY-MIB-FILE", "myAlarmServiceProvider"),))

    myAlarmEntry = MibTableRow((1, 3, 6, 1, 4, 1, xxxx, x, xx, x, xxx, x, x, x, 1)).setIndexNames((0, "MY-MIB-FILE", "myAlarmId"))
    if mibBuilder.loadTexts: myAlarmEntry.setDescription("A row containing one alarm.")

    myAlarmTime = MibTableColumn((1, 3, 6, 1, 4, 1, xxxx, x, xx, x, xxx, x, x, 1, 1, 9), DateAndTime()).setMaxAccess("readonly")
    if mibBuilder.loadTexts: myAlarmTime.setDescription("This variable is the time of this Alarm object.")

DateAndTime was imported from SNMPv2-TC.


 One update for this:
Even the exception shows the problem is caused by 'myAlarmTime'

    pysnmp.smi.error.SmiError: Instance index (0,) to OID convertion failure at object 'myAlarmTime': ConstraintsIntersection(ConstraintsIntersection(ConstraintsIntersection(), ValueRangeConstraint(-2147483648, 2147483647)), ValueRangeConstraint(1, 2147483647)) failed at: "ValueRangeConstraint(1, 2147483647) failed at: "0"" at Integer32

But after debug, it seems the real issue is 'myAlarmId' syntax definition. As that variable is the only one defined with Integer32 with range (1, 2147483647)

    myAlarmId = MibTableColumn((1, 3, 6, 1, 4, 1, xxxx, x, xx, x, xxx, x, 4, 1, 1, 1),
                                Integer32().subtype(subtypeSpec=ValueRangeConstraint(1, 2147483647))).setMaxAccess("readonly")

And after I change its value range to (0, 2147483647), the 
    instanceIndex = (0,)
start to works.

But I should NOT change that right?
Then what should be the root cause for this issue?
Pysnmp bug?
or our misunderstanding for SNMP and MIB file? 

BTW: the suffix .0 was working fine with a Java based SNMP cilent. But due to company's order and license issue, we need to re-implement it and we though python should be a good enough to do this.

Attach more MIB definition relate to this alarm event

            myAlarmServiceProvider			DisplayString,
            myAlarmSecurityAlarmDetector		DisplayString,
            myAlarmADMC			INTEGER
        }

issue_title: underlying error if raised while setting password in keyring is not raised.
issue_body:
In the below code if OSError is raised then actually reason for the error is never known.

keyring/backend.py:
    
    class _ExtensionKeyring(KeyringBackend):
        def set_password(self, service, username, password):
            """Overide the set_password() in KeyringBackend.
            """
        try:
            self.keyring_impl.password_set(service, username, password)
        except OSError:
            raise PasswordSetError()
    
So i think we should change   this to.
    
        try:
            self.keyring_impl.password_set(service, username, password)
        except OSError, e:
            raise PasswordSetError(e)
    
so that we will know whats the actually error is.


regards,

Moqtar

issue_title: depth argument of Cell.get_labels() is confusing
issue_body:
I'm referring to the following code segment.

```python
    def get_labels(self, depth=None):
        labels = libCopy.deepcopy(self.labels)
        if depth is None or depth >= 0:
            for element in self.elements:
                if isinstance(element, CellReference):
                    labels.extend(
                        element.get_labels(None if depth is None else depth -
                                           1))
                elif isinstance(element, CellArray):
                    labels.extend(
                        element.get_labels(None if depth is None else depth -
                                           1))
        return labels
```

Passing in depth=0, one gets labels from the top-level cell, plus labels from the first-level child elements.
In order to get labels from the top-level cell only, one has to pass in depth=-1. 

This behavior was confusing to my colleagues. After reading the code, I think it's better to change one line:
```python
        if depth is None or depth > 0:
```

issue_title: GDS Timestamps
issue_body:
The present implementation seems to use current date and time for GDS cell and library timestamps. As a result, GDS files created from the same layout at different points in time will have different binary headers. While appropriate in most situations, this behaviour complicates some use cases that involve binary comparison of GDS files (e.g. regression testing).

I would like to propose to add an optional `timestamp=None` argument to `Cell.to_gds()`, `GdsLibrary.write_gds()`, and `GdsWriter()` constructor. If set to `None` (the default), the functions would use the current date and time, otherwise the user-supplied timestamp would be used.

issue_title: To use that great extension with windows ...
issue_body:
I had to do the following modifications:

1. Install the msvc for python [https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/download/details.aspx?id=44266](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/download/details.aspx?id=44266)
2. Adjust the setup.py a) to not use stdlibc++, b) to use cythonize (from Cython.Build import cythonize and in kwargs change ext_module to 'ext_modules':  cythonize(extensions),  c) import numpy and add include_dirs=[numpy.get_include()], in extensions:
```
from setuptools import setup, find_packages, Extension
from Cython.Build import cythonize
import os.path
import warnings
import numpy

classifiers = [
    'Programming Language :: Python :: 2',
    'Programming Language :: Python :: 3',
    'Intended Audience :: Science/Research',
    'License :: OSI Approved :: MIT License',
    'Topic :: Scientific/Engineering'
]

extensions = [Extension(
        'fastdtw._fastdtw',
        [os.path.join('fastdtw', "_fastdtw.pyx")],
        language="c++",
        include_dirs=[numpy.get_include()],
#       if you want to debug from MSVC IDE uncomment the following
#        extra_compile_args=["-Zi", "/Od"],
#        extra_link_args=["-debug"],

#        libraries=["stdc++","stdc"]
    )]

kwargs = {
    'name': 'fastdtw',
    'version': '0.3.0',
    'author': 'Kazuaki Tanida',
    'url': 'https://github.com/slaypni/fastdtw',
    'description': 'Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) algorithm with an O(N) time and memory complexity.',
    'license': 'MIT',
    'keywords': ['dtw'],
    'install_requires': ['numpy'],
    'packages': find_packages(),
    'ext_modules':  cythonize(extensions),
    'test_suite': 'tests',
    'setup_requires': ['pytest-runner'],
    'tests_require': ['pytest'],
    'classifiers': classifiers
}

try:
    setup(**kwargs)
except SystemExit:
    del kwargs['ext_modules']
    warnings.warn('compilation failed. Installing pure python package')
    setup(**kwargs)
```

3. Modify the cpx to use the INFINITY definition from numpy: 
```
#from libc.math cimport INFINITY,pow, fabs
from libc.math cimport pow, fabs
from numpy.math cimport INFINITY
```

Thank you for your great work!

Jan

issue_title: 4scanner hashing all image even with ` "check_duplicate": false `
issue_body:
The hash file is created even when ` "check_duplicate" ` is set to false in the json config file.
This is a problem because the point of this option is to not create a hash file because it may be too slow (for example if you have 1 million picture in your download folder, or if you are downloading to slow nfs drive etc...)

To fix the issue, this function in download.py must only be called if ` "check_duplicate" ` is true:
` dupecheck.hash_img_in_folder(out_dir, img_hash_log) `

def start_recording(recorded_events_queue=None, times_list = False):
    """
    Starts recording all keyboard events into a global variable, or the given
    queue if any. Returns the queue of events and the hooked function.
    Use `stop_recording()` or `unhook(hooked_function)` to stop.

    recorded_events_queue = recorded_events_queue or _queue.Queue()

It's come up for a sorting use case, so the example below uses that, but the reload that should now happen from `helpers.set_bedtools_path()` only seems to work when invoking  `pybedtools.bedtool.BedTool()` rather than  `pybedtools.BedTool()`. When bedtools is *not* on the path, the first try case will fail:

```python
import pybedtools

issue_title: nodeenv --list is raising TypeError
issue_body:
When installed globally with `sudo pip install nodeenv`, `nodeenv --list` raises an exception.

This is due to `pkg_resources.parse_version` method having different return types in older versions. 

![nodeenv_bug](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6031925/6599860/d253a2b0-c814-11e4-94d6-9a9d79cdf27c.png)


Possible hotfix solutions are:
* write a helper function to parse strings,
* write our own Version type :-1: 

But atm I'm working on finding out which version of `setuptools` introduced the newer (currently used in master) Version type that parses version strings and rely on it if `setuptools` is >= that version.

I'll combine that with one of two approaches:
* the raw string representation we get with regex pattern. However ascending sorting will be `0.1.0, 0.10.0, 0.9.0`,
*  the before mentioned helper function


I think broken ascending sort is acceptable as a hotfix.

PR will be submitted soon-ish. :8ball:

issue_title: Freezegun doesn't work after calling other frozen methods
issue_body:
When decorating a class with a frozen time, if a method calls out to another method on the class, it will cause the patcher to stop and undo the frozen time for the rest of the initial calling method. This appears to only happen when ```from datetime import datetime``` is called as opposed to ```import datetime```.

For example:

```python
from datetime import datetime
from freezegun import freeze_time
import unittest


@freeze_time('1980-01-01')
class FooTest(object):
    def test_bar(self):
        datetime.now() # returns 1980-01-01 00:00:00
        self.other_func() 
        datetime.now() # returns 2015-04-21 07:03:00

    def other_func(self):
        # do stuff
```

issue_title: discord.Permission.manage_roles is True if the role it belongs to has the new Administrator permission
No error returned, but process not completing when calling replace_roles.
issue_body:
Hello, I'm in the process of building a mute feature for a bot.  I'm using the API available through pip on python3.6, for clarity.

I have the problem narrowed down to the area in which I replace the roles.  The user exists, and so does the 'Muted' role, however, upon calling `replace_roles(member, *roles)`, no error is thrown, but the roles are not replaced.  I have tried using `remove_roles` by iterating over the user's roles, but this did not work either.  I saw in another thread in the issues that one is supposed to use replace instead of remove in this  instance.

The flow of logic is as follows:
```
if message.content.startswith("..mute"):
    # For getting the user's ID, as the command is used with a mention of the user
    to_mute = message.content.split(" ")[1][2:-1]
    member = discord.utils.find(lambda member: member.id == to_mute, message.channel.server.members)
    if member is not None:
        role = discord.utils.find(lambda role: role.name == 'Muted', message.channel.server.roles)
        if role is not None:
            client.replace_roles(member, role)
            tmp = yield from client.send_message(message.channel, "User muted!")
```

At which point, if I iterate over `member.roles`, it still retains all the old roles, despite printing that the user was muted.  This will likely prove to be an issue later when I am writing in a self-assignable roles feature.  Is there an piece of syntax I am missing?

issue_title: cache_auth file is world readable (security concern)
issue_body:
discord.py caches a token for server logins. On my Linux system the cache file is in `/tmp/discord_py` and both the directory and the file are world readable. That means anyone with access to my Linux system could use my Discord login's credentials.

I think the solution is to change [the code that creates the cache file](https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/blob/async/discord/client.py#L200-L203) to make the file only readable by the owner. It'd be nice to use the `tempfile` module in Python to create the file, but I'm not sure it's quite suitable. Using `os.chmod()` to make the file mode 0600 should work but then that needs a bit of testing on non-Unix platforms.

I'm filing this issue now to document it. I should be able to contribute a pull request to close it, but it may take a few days.

issue_title: server: Write test for spawning subprocesses in workers
issue_body:
Spawning additional subprocesses in the child workers when `use_threading=True` because only the main thread can receive signals from child processes.
[This line](https://github.com/achimnol/aiotools/blob/0667c2d/aiotools/server.py#L211) instantiates a child watcher before launching workers, as described in [the Python docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-subprocess.html#subprocess-and-threads).

Currently there is no explicit test case that tests if this actually works. Let's add one.

issue_title: Metrics implementation speed concern
issue_body:
Hi, took a look at the layout for metrics. Looks interesting, little too Java-style, but can't be helped.
Problem is, that the current sensor implementation looks quite heavy for Python. You know, numbers, it's bad at it.
Question: _What I wan't to clarify is the role of metrics. Are they purely optional or will also be used for internal tasks?_
By the look of it, it's not really optional, sensors are called, doing some work. If metrics are needed to serve an internal cause (like a must for quota fixes or something) we should benchmark it and optimize. If it's just for users to install their own, maybe we should do something like:
```Python
if metrics_installed:
    self._sensors.records_fetch_lag.record(highwater - last_offset)
```
to minimize the impact. I didn't benchmark it yet, but in Python classes will always add overhead and metrics will only increase with time.

issue_title: Tablesorter sometimes not being loaded into jQuery with require()
issue_body:
On 2.30.3, we've occasionally had a very odd issue, where Tablesorter is loaded, but not added to jQuery.fn, so that we end up with "tablesorter is not a function" errors.

```coffee
$ = require('jquery')
tablesorter = require('tablesorter')

settings = {
  theme: 'materialize'
}

$(document).on 'load', () ->
  window.tablesorter = tablesorter

  $("table.sortable").each (index, element) ->
      $(element).tablesorter(settings) # sometimes fails with a "$(...).tablesorter is not a function"
```

It seems like it's a weird dependency/build order thing, because sometimes it works, sometimes it doesn't, but in every case, `window.tablesorter` is set. We haven't found a way to solve this issue permanently, except by changing the way we initialise tablesorter, to basically duplicate the existing $.fn = in tablesorter (yuck!):

```coffee
  $("table.sortable").each (index, element) ->
    # merge & extend config options
    c = $.extend( true, {}, tablesorter.defaults, settings, tablesorter.instanceMethods )
    # save initial settings
    c.originalSettings = settings
    # create a table from data (build table widget)
    tablesorter.setup element, c
```

I'm wondering if it's possible/sensible to add a method to Tablesorter that allows it to be setup on an arbitrary element, without needing to go though $/jQuery? What do you think?

For example:

```coffee
$ = require('jquery')
tablesorter = require('tablesorter')

$(document).on 'load', () ->
  $("table.sortable").each (index, element) ->
    tablesorter.apply(element)  # or tablesorter.apply($, element)
```

issue_title: Safari + cssStickyHeaders + Caption, makes table grow to infinity
issue_body:
not sure why, but with a caption, $table.height(); starts to grow by a multiple of scroll size after the height of caption.  This seems to fix it but I haven't tested it outside of safari nor do I know if it's the best fix for this.  I basically set a var tableH and use that till we pass a certain point.  At the bottom of the table I get a little jumpiness but it corrects it's self, it's better then scrolling into infinite whiteness ;)

```
diff --git a/js/widgets/widget-cssStickyHeaders.js b/js/widgets/widget-cssStickyHeaders.js
index 07ed0f66..513ddcd9 100644
--- a/js/widgets/widget-cssStickyHeaders.js
+++ b/js/widgets/widget-cssStickyHeaders.js
@@ -29,6 +29,8 @@
                                $parent = $table.parent().closest('table.' + ts.css.table),
                                $parentThead = $parent.length && ts.hasWidget($parent[0], 'cssStickyHeaders') ? $parent.children('thead') : [],
                                borderTopWidth = ( parseInt( $table.css('border-top-width'), 10 ) || 0 ),
+                               // Fixes for safari
+                               tableH = $table.height(),
                                lastCaptionSetting = wo.cssStickyHeaders_addCaption,
                                // table offset top changes while scrolling in FF
                                adjustOffsetTop = false,
@@ -71,6 +73,12 @@
                                        adjustY = $table.offset().top;
                                }
 
+                               // Fix for safari, when caption present, table
+                               // height changes while scrolling
+                               if ($win.scrollTop() < $caption.outerHeight(true)) {
+                                       tableH = $table.height();
+                               }
+
                                var top = $attach.length ? $attach.offset().top : $win.scrollTop(),
                                // add caption height; include table padding top & border-spacing or text may be above the fold (jQuery UI themes)
                                // border-spacing needed in Firefox, but not webkit... not sure if I should account for that
@@ -78,7 +86,7 @@
                                        ( parseInt( $table.css('padding-top'), 10 ) || 0 ) +
                                        ( parseInt( $table.css('border-spacing'), 10 ) || 0 ),
 
-                               bottom = $table.height() + ( addCaptionHeight && wo.cssStickyHeaders_addCaption ? captionHeight : 0 ) -
+                               bottom = tableH + ( addCaptionHeight && wo.cssStickyHeaders_addCaption ? captionHeight : 0 ) -
                                        $thead.height() - ( $table.children('tfoot').height() || 0 ) -
                                        ( wo.cssStickyHeaders_addCaption ? captionHeight : ( addCaptionHeight ? 0 : captionHeight ) ),
 
```

issue_title: Configurable named times (breakfast, tonight, etc)
issue_body:
Some of the named times are rather subjective (e.g. *breakfast*, *afternoon*) while others are not (e.g. *noon*, *midnight*). It would be great to be able to allow my users to configure, for example, what time *breakfast* (*desayuno*, *Frühstück*, etc) means to them. In my use case, tasks are being recorded with future due dates and reminders.

Currently these are stored in the locale, so there would be no reasonable way to change the time designated to a term across multiple languages. That *might* be okay since switching locales would be uncommon, but it is not optimal. I could handle this by presenting the user with the keys in locale's `re_sources` dict and allowing the time value for any phrases that happen to be there to be changed, but my app has no semantic understanding of what those phrases represent.

It may be more useful to map those to named constants so that the locale is represented consistently across locales as `'breakfast': source_times.BREAKFAST` and `'desayuno': source_times.BREAKFAST`. Those constants may be simple time values as the locales are currently configured, or functions that given a date return a time.

It is important to decide whether these terms represent the same time every day or if on certain dates (most commonly certain days of the week) the times are different. If I really want something to notify me "at breakfast time" when I am literally at the table eating then I would want to configure "breakfast" as 6:30am weekdays, 7:30am weekends. I would need to hear from others that this is useful before considering implementing it over a simple static *breakfast* is *6:30AM* mapping.

issue_title: Error loading configuration file for multiple-argument option with append action
issue_body:
There is an issue when loading a configuration file for an option that allows multiple arguments (`nargs` > 1) and has the `action` set to `append`. For example, consider this script called `test.py`:
```
import configargparse

p = configargparse.ArgParser(
    config_file_parser_class=configargparse.YAMLConfigFileParser,
    args_for_writing_out_config_file=['-w', '--write-out-config-file']
)
p.add('-c', '--config', is_config_file=True, help='config file path')
p.add('-i', '--indexes', type=int, metavar=('x', 'y'), nargs=2,
      action='append')

options = p.parse_args()

print(options)
print("----------")
print(p.format_help())
print("----------")
print(p.format_values())
```
and this sample configuration file `test.yaml`:
```
indexes:
- - 2
  - 4
- - 1
  - 9
```
Running the script as follows:
```
python test.py -c test.yaml
```
gives the following error message:
```
usage: test.py [-h] [-w CONFIG_OUTPUT_PATH] [-c CONFIG] [-i x y]
test.py: error: argument -i/--indexes: expected 2 arguments
```
I had expected `configargparse` to correctly load the configuration file.

issue_title: Can't decode 0xa9, which is the copyright symbol
issue_body:
This is actually more of a problem with Python's UTF8 code, I think. But if you run the trufflehog script against [this repo](https://github.com/dullroar/TW5-atomfeed), you'll get the following error:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/lehmer/Sandbox/trufflehog.py", line 130, in <module>
    project_path = find_strings(args.git_url)
  File "/home/lehmer/Sandbox/trufflehog.py", line 95, in find_strings
    printableDiff = blob.diff.decode()
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/encodings/utf_8.py", line 16, in decode
    return codecs.utf_8_decode(input, errors, True)
UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf8' codec can't decode byte 0xa9 in position 96: invalid start byte
```

This is because an older version of the license file had a copyright symbol (0xa9, which is the rendered value of the HTML entity ©, for example). I figured it out by putting a `print(blob.diff)` right before line 95.

issue_title: Suggested backwards-incompatible change - LASFile.curves to go from list to ordered dictionary
issue_body:
I am thinking about making a backwards-incompatible change where the ``LASFile.curves`` attribute (currently a list of ``Curve`` objects) becomes an ``OrderedDictionary`` object, where the key is the curve mnemonic and the value is the ``Curve`` object.

This would make accessing all of the LAS file header section attributes identical -- ``version``, ``well``, and ``params`` are already ``OrderedDictionary`` objects. It would also allow me some other fixes and enhancements that would be a lot easier and make more sense if I make this change. Also much better internal structure looking towards LAS 3.0 support

Any comments? 

I bring it up because it would break existing code. 

I could support both by having ``curves_list`` and ``curves_dict`` attributes, and then ``curves`` could be either one -- I would leave it as a list for now and then in the future swap it over to dict. That would get me all of the benefits of the internal changes and less confusion for users.

issue_title: Visualising MNIST Filters
issue_body:

I'm comparing activation maximisation used here from the the one described here: https://blog.keras.io/how-convolutional-neural-networks-see-the-world.html

issue_title: i bet we can center and scale even sparse data manually.
issue_body:
since we're doing this with dictionaries, it should be possible. 

here's the approach (will implement slightly differently). 

1. iterate through our list of dicts
2. for each one that has the feature name we're looking for, save that data
3. now we have a dense list that is all the actual values that exist for that column. 
4. from there, it's pretty easy to calculate whatever min/max and center values we want (i prefer 90th percentile and 10th percentile, with a tiny bit of logic to handle 0's and such). 
5. this all happens inside fit, obviously
6. then in transform, we check each property in the passed-in dictionary against it's relevant scale. we, of course, do not try to do anything for properties that do not exist on that dictionary. 

et voila, we have scaling! 

implementation details:
min/max scaler is obviously easiest to implement. but i'm still super drawn to 90th and 10th percentile. 
clearly holding all this data in memory would be... expensive. 
so, we could try a couple different approaches to memory management. 
1. iterate through the list repeatedly, grabbing the values for only n/3 columns at a time (where n is the total number of columns, as calculated by a feature i've yet to implement in BasicFeatureFormatting). 
2. We could grab only a random quarter of the rows.

issue_title: Fail to connect to X Server with error "TypeError: 'type' object does not support ..."
issue_body:
Hi

I have been using PyAutoGui to connect to X servers running on a LAN.  I use a work around I found to enable this to happen.  It's worked fine up until recently with the introduction of a new remote computer.  A cut down version of the code I use is shown below;

```
import pyautogui
import Xlib

class g:
#    otmXDisplay = "192.168.1.31:0.0"
    otmXDisplay = "10.60.102.11:0.0"

pyautogui.platformModule._display = Xlib.display.Display (g.otmXDisplay)
```
When connecting to all remote X servers previously this has always worked fine.  The new computer fails with the following Python error;

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 29, in <module>
    pyautogui.platformModule._display = Xlib.display.Display (g.otmXDisplay)
  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/Xlib/display.py", line 127, in __init__
  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/Xlib/ext/randr.py", line 1191, in init
  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/Xlib/display.py", line 334, in extension_add_subevent
  File "build/bdist.linux-x86_64/egg/Xlib/protocol/display.py", line 329, in add_extension_event
TypeError: 'type' object does not support item assignment
```
I've done my best to look at display.py to see what might be going wrong, but my Python skills are not sufficient to make sense of what I see.

I have taken to a spot of Wiresharking and I can see the following on a working system (this is really cut down, I can post the whole thing if required);
```
Client                               Server
Initial Connection Request ->
                                    <- Reply
GetKeyboardMapping ->
                                    <- Reply
ListExtensions ->
                                    <- Reply
QueryExtension ->                             Repeated for each of the extensions
                                    <- Reply
```
The working connection attempt has 10 extensions, each of which are queried.  I can see that the connection that fails also has a similar list (not sure if there are 10 identical, but they're similar).

The order that they're listed in the "ListExtensions" reply is different on both systems but this does not worry me (not sure why!)

The one that fails always does so on the following one... Query "RANDR".  This is a hand-written summary of a comparison of the two replies;
```
Field                       Fails                      Working
reply                       1                          1
unused
sequencenumber              7                          9
reply length                0                          0
present                  True                         True
major-opcode             139                             140
first-event               88                           89
first-error                145                         147

```
The only thing I can see there is that the op-codes are not the same; is that important?  Not sure.  The response "COMPOSITE" is a different op-code too... 142 on the working system and 140 on the one that fails.

If anyone has any ideas about why these different responses seem to result in a failed connection, I'd be really grateful for your insight.

Thanks

Dave

issue_title: Support setting window title in hello_world.py example
issue_body:
On Windows this it is easy to set window title, as SetPopup has a windowName parameter. On Mac and Linux CEF doesn't support setting window title through CefWindowInfo. I've tried to use CEF views in hello_world.py example, however it didn't work on Linux. Reported issue on CEF Forum: http://www.magpcss.org/ceforum/viewtopic.php?f=6&t=15006 . 

The solution for Mac and Linux is to call native Cocoa / X11 functions to set window title.

issue_title: Pygrok New API
issue_body:
From v1.0, Pygrok will make big performance enhancement and the API will change, probably new API looks like this:

```
from pygrok import Grok

pattern = 'load average: %{NUMBER:load_1:float}, %{NUMBER:load_2:float}, %{NUMBER:load_3:float}'

text = 'load average: 1.88, 1.73, 1.49'

grok = Grok(pattern)

m = grok.match(text)
``` 

This will output parsed data: 
```
{'load_1': 1.88, 'load_2': 1.73, 'load_3': 1.49}
```

With precompiled regex pattern inside, you can invoke `grok.match` thousands of time without too much performance loss.This will address #3.

issue_title: Exclusive groups are repeated on the compilation
issue_body:
When adding exclusive groups, the autocompletion will continue to offer all the members of the group although only one of them can be chosen.
Example:
```
#!/usr/bin/env python
import argparse
import argcomplete

parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()

group = parser.add_mutually_exclusive_group()
group.add_argument('--test', action='store_true')
group.add_argument('--no_test', action='store_true')
argcomplete.autocomplete(parser, exclude=['-h', '--help'])
args = parser.parse_args()
```
Expected:
```
$ ./test.py <tab>
--test --no_test
$ ./test.py --test <tab>
```

I could not found a solution here, when I used my code (PR #143), I got close, but because it's exclusive, if I chose --test no I will get forever --no_test on the compilation because it's always False.

issue_title: [docs] clarify recomendation for `PYTHON_ARGCOMPLETE_OK` declaration
issue_body:
I was initially under the impression that argcompleter would load my python module and check if its namespace _contained_ a 'PYTHON_ARGCOMPLETE_OK' attribute, as in the docs it is only mentioned that this key is needed, so for now i'm running with scissors declaring `PYTHON_ARGCOMPLETE_OK = True` in my module's constants block. However its possible that this form could be pushed down the file outside of that 1024-byte head that is mentioned is scanned. I would ask that the docs give a clear example of how this is expected to be used. I considered this as an option:

```
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# pragma: PYTHON_ARGCOMPLETE_OK

""" Header Doc
"""
...
```
(oh I also found that touching this text at all seemed to break argcomplete until I opened a new shell, so I haven't actually tested that this form works, I am a bit gunshy of touching it as every time I do it stops working) 

though I'm not sure it would operate well with shell-style header docs, this may assumedly needs to come after a copyright notice, witch often use the shell-style header docs (though often this is only used as a comment on the source itself, not on the module docstring, so having it appear in that shell-style header might be totally fine). I dunno, I suppose I would think it really neat if argcomplete could check the namespace of function invoked by setuptool's console_script for a specific attribute; then there aren't conflicts with needing to be within a specific byte range of the file - but I also haven't thought of an implementation that could achieve that generically.

So i dunno, would be nice to have more examples of the different supported argcomplete schemes. Maybe even embed some example projects simmilar to what the pypa/sampleproject does.

Thank you

issue_title: Issue with telephone() of provider Personal()
issue_body:
So, it's returns too many similar data. We need to fix it **immediately**.

This method is based on the `custom_code()` method of the `Code()` provider and it should works like `custom_code()`, but it doesn't.

How works `telephone()` now:

```python
>>> from mimesis import Personal

>>> s = {Personal('ru').telephone() for _ in range(2000)}
{'+7-(967)-072-06-28', '+7-(915)-272-71-92', '+7-(937)-072-06-28', ... }
>>> len(s)
14 # Only 14 unique values from 2000.
```
How it should work:

```python
>>> import random

>>> from mimesis import Code
>>> mask = random.choice([
    "+7-(96#)-###-##-##",
    "+7-(92#)-###-##-##",
    "+7-(93#)-###-##-##",
    "+7-(91#)-###-##-##",
    "+7-(999)-###-##-##",
    "+7-(9##)-###-##-##",
    "+7-(909)-###-##-##",
    "+7-(968)-###-##-##"
])

>>> c = {Code('ru').custom_code(mask=mask) for _ in range(2000)}
{'+7-(921)-395-52-94', '+7-(955)-222-89-60', '+7-(978)-727-56-77', ... }
>>> len(c)
2000 # All values unique.
```
Similar problems are observed with other methods, such as `identifier()`.

P.S As i can judge, the problem not in a `seed`.

Thanks for the great job. When experimented with backtrader, I can easily manipulate the single instrument (say one stock). I'm wondering if there's an way (without using too many for loop) to do cross section analysis in each next().

For example, if every month, I want to buy & hold the top10 stocks from a pool of 100 stocks based on certain criteria: say Past 1 Month's return. Currently the way I can think of is to:

seraphic

issue_title: justext: previous call leaks into the parser
issue_body:
There is an issue (really lxml's fault) with `core.justext()` whereby, lxml uses a "global" (module-level) HTML parser in `lxml.html.fromstring()` by default if a parser is not passed.  When you pass "partial" html, then it is fed to the global parser, and in subsequent calls continue to feed the global parser.  I'm not sure if this is a "bug" in lxml or a feature.  However it affects gettext in that it gives unexpected and non-deterministic results::

    >>> justext.justext('<justext></justext>', justext.get_stoplist('English'))
    ➥  []
    >>> justext.justext('', justext.get_stoplist('English'))
      File "<string>", line unknown
    XMLSyntaxError: line 1: Tag justext invalid

This can be worked around by passing a new instance to `lxml.html.fromstring()` whenever it is called:

```diff
diff --git a/justext/core.py b/justext/core.py
index bc34691..2dbe75f 100644
--- a/justext/core.py
+++ b/justext/core.py
@@ -60,11 +60,11 @@ def html_to_dom(html, default_encoding=DEFAULT_ENCODING, encoding=None, errors=D
         decoded_html = decode_html(html, default_encoding, encoding, errors)
 
     try:
-        dom = lxml.html.fromstring(decoded_html)
+        dom = lxml.html.fromstring(decoded_html, parser=lxml.html.HTMLParser())
     except ValueError:
         # Unicode strings with encoding declaration are not supported.
         # for XHTML files with encoding declaration, use the declared encoding
-        dom = lxml.html.fromstring(html)
+        dom = lxml.html.fromstring(html, parser=lxml.html.HTMLParser())
 
     return dom

```

    >>> import justext
    >>> justext.justext('<justext></justext>', justext.get_stoplist('English'))
    ➥  []
    >>> justext.justext('', justext.get_stoplist('English'))
      File "<string>", line unknown
    XMLSyntaxError

In this (contrived) example we still get an error, but it's somewhat expected and deterministic.

issue_title: Coveralls isn't recognising information in uploads
issue_body:
Hi, i've started using this great proyect some weeks ago. It's a great proyect very easy to use!.

Since last sunday (31/5), coveralls stoped showing me last code coverage updates. All uploads are marked as if there are no valid files ([build 77](https://coveralls.io/builds/2692982), [build 78](https://coveralls.io/builds/2693648), [build 79](https://coveralls.io/builds/2694436), [build 80](https://coveralls.io/builds/2695006), [build 81](https://coveralls.io/builds/2695243), [build 82](https://coveralls.io/builds/2695466), [build 83](https://coveralls.io/builds/2695527) ). I've not change `cpp-coveralls` command line configuration, so i really don't know if it's coveralls fault, mine o other thing.

I'm uploading data using [this `.travis.yml`](https://github.com/NickCis/7552-taller-prog-2-2015-C1/blob/master/.travis.yml), bassically the line executed is this:

    coveralls -i src -i test -e src/coverage.cpp -E '.*\/mongoose\/mongoose\.[hc]' -E '.*CMakeFiles/feature_tests\..*' -E '.*CMakeCXXCompilerId\.cpp' -E '.*CMakeCCompilerId\.c' -E '.*\/test\/.*\.(h|hpp|cpp)$' -r ./

The proyect is usign `CMake`, under the folder `build` the main program is built and in the folder `test` test binaries are built. All the binaries are compiled with gcov flags and executed.

Lastest build is [this one](https://travis-ci.org/NickCis/7552-taller-prog-2-2015-C1/builds/64865389) and the last line of the output of `cpp-coveralls` is this:

    {u'url': u'https://coveralls.io/jobs/6323819', u'message': u'Job #82.1'}

If i execute the command in my machine and dump `cpp-coveralls` to a json i get [this output](https://gist.github.com/NickCis/c04333f6947bc1e00de6). And the last line of `cpp-coveralls` is this one:

    {u'url': u'https://coveralls.io/jobs/6323949', u'message': u'Job #83.1'}

As far as i understand all the files are in that json. If i upload the result locally i get the same problem with no valid files.

The coverall page of the proyect is: https://coveralls.io/r/NickCis/7552-taller-prog-2-2015-C1

Thanks in advance!

issue_title: underscore prefix on functions does not prevent an 'unused function' warning.
issue_body:
I'm using the Python library Flask for developing routes.

Here is my route:

```
@app.route("/status", methods=['GET'])
def status():
    return "OK", 200
```

This yields this warning:

```
server.py:66: unused function 'status'
```

If I add an underscore prefix to the status function name, I get this warning:

```
server.py:66: unused function '_status'
```

Since the function name is not important to Flask, I can just call the function '_' which yields

```
server.py:66: unused function '_'
```

Is there some way of avoiding this warning?

Thanks!

issue_title: SQL injections are possible when using partition string_firstchars (Postgres, Django)
issue_body:
`SUBSTR(LOWER(NEW.<column_name>), 1, <n>)` is used in the trigger function. If the column value contains special characters it breaks. And if you chose n large enough SQL injections become possible. We fixed this using:
```SUBSTR(LOWER(regexp_replace(NEW.<column_name>, '[^a-zA-Z]', '', 'g')), 1, <n>);```
Which makes everything much more stable

issue_title: If statements coerces objects to boolean
issue_body: Best described by example: In js: ```js var Mustache = require('mustache') var body = '{{#foobar}}\n' + ' {{#link}}\n' + ' {{ text}}\n' + ' {{/link}}\n' + ' {{^link}}\n' + ' {{ . }}\n' + ' {{/link}}\n' + '{{/foobar}}'; var values = [ 'foo', 'bar', {'link': '/', 'text': 'link test'}, {'link': '/x', 'text': 'x'}, 'spam' ] ``` console.log(Mustache.render(body, {'foobar': values})) ``` foo bar link test x spam ``` In python using pystache and chevron: ```python import pystache import chevron body = """ {{#foobar}} {{#link}} {{ text}} {{/link}} {{^link}} {{ . }} {{/link}} {{/foobar}} """ values = [ 'foo', 'bar', {'link': '/', 'text': 'link test'}, {'link': '/x', 'text': 'x'}, 'spam' ] print('pystache:') print(pystache.render(body, {'foobar': values})) print('chevron:') print(chevron.render(body, {'foobar': values})) ``` Output: ``` pystache: foo bar link test x spam chevron: True True link test x True ``` The values of the list are displayed as bools if the if statement `{{#link}}` is included. Obviously, without that the values display properly. I've no idea what the spec says about this but I think chrevon should behave the same as mustache.js.

issue_title: NowPlaying 'live' thumbnail url
issue_body:
Feature enhancement to provide a method of retrieving a transcode thumbnail of a video.

The NowPlaying view of Plex provides an updating view of a playing video. This is simply a transcoded (resized) frame at an offset into the video file.

Here's an example url: 

`http://192.168.1.211:32400/photo/:/transcode?url=http://127.0.0.1:32400/library/parts/239414/indexes/sd/3323849&width=700&height=394&minSize=1`

The embedded url `(http://192.168.1.211:32400/library/parts/239414/indexes/sd/3319849`) is the offset frame into the video stream. The /status/sessions Video viewOffset attribute can be used as the index. (eg. `viewOffset="3319849"`). 

It would be nice if there were two methods to do something similar in plexapi - 

1. plexserver.sessions() - could return an object that contains a url for transcoding an frame at the current offset into the video stream

2. a new utility function - provided a stream id and offset could return a transcode url

Lastly it might also be nice to have a method of retrieving the url as a png file.

I know this can be pieced together from the contents of sessions(), etc. But, for applications that don't have url formatting capabilities or don't support mechanisms for http GET processing - it might be nice to provide these convenience functions.

issue_title: Issue with betamax not recording iter_xxx generator requests
issue_body:
cf sigmavirus24/github3.py#679

## tl;dr

I have setup tests with helpers such as:

``` python
class TestTheThing:
    def helper_method(self, arg1, arg2, arg3):
        with self.recorder.use_cassette("cassette_name"):
            thing_to_test = self.thing_containing_what_to_test(arg1)
            return self.thing_to_test.iter_items(arg2, arg3)

    def test_with_some_options(self):
        contents = self.helper_method("a", "b", "c")
        assert list(contents) == ['foobar']
```

in the example above, I'm returning a generator from the helper method. So when in the test I'm transforming it into a list, the iterator runs the generator **AFTER** the `with statement` has been exited.

The fix is simply to run the generator *within* the `with` block context.

## possible actions

* keep it as is for future readers
* make it as part of a "stupid mistake" FAQ?
* add some parameter to betamax to help debug that class of mistakes faster

issue_title: Support readline or equivalent alternatives
issue_body:
Googling leads me to [this SO question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/45659928/readline-python-module-does-not-work-with-aioconsole-ainput) but there is no reply yet.

Python already provides a binding module to GNU readline or libedit, but this does not work together with aioconsole since it does not have coroutine-based interfaces. Maybe we need a new lower-level wrapper for the readline library.

This issue is both question and feature request:
 * Is there any plan to support readline or readline-style interactive prompts?
 * Are there any known/fundamental technical issues related to supporting readline (except that the stdlib readline does not have coroutines)?
   - Could we just make the user prompt to be synchronous while leaving user codes running between prompts asynchronous?

issue_title: Make storage adapter query support true by default in a future release
issue_body:
At the moment, not all of ChatterBot's storage backends support querying and filters. To maintain backwards compatability, a field (`adapter_supports_queries`) has been included on all storage adapters. By default, `adapter_supports_queries` is false. If a storage adapter supports queries, then it needs to have `adapter_supports_queries = True` set in it's `__init__` method.

In some future release, storage adapters should be set to support queries by default.

issue_title: Creation and initialization of the relay SQLite3 database are not atomic
issue_body:
In database.py, `get_db`:

```
    must_create = (dbfile == ":memory:") or not os.path.exists(dbfile)
    try:
        db = sqlite3.connect(dbfile)
    except (EnvironmentError, sqlite3.OperationalError) as e:
        raise DBError("Unable to create/open db file %s: %s" % (dbfile, e))
    db.row_factory = dict_factory
    db.execute("PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON")
    problems = db.execute("PRAGMA foreign_key_check").fetchall()
    if problems:
        raise DBError("failed foreign key check: %s" % (problems,))

    if must_create:
        log.msg("populating new database with schema v%s" % target_version)
        schema = get_schema(target_version)
        db.executescript(schema)
        db.execute("INSERT INTO version (version) VALUES (?)",
                   (target_version,))
        db.commit()
```

If no database exists and the process crashes any time after `sqlite3.connect` but before `db.commit` then the system ends up in an inconsistent state from which it cannot recover without manual intervention - a database file exists but has no schema.  Subsequent attempts to open it will fail, most likely with an error about the `version` table not existing.

For example:

```
starting wormhole relay server

issue_title: bbox to GeoJSON spec
Documentation on testing pyshp
issue_body:
This library only seems like it was designed to be tested during installation.  Since, the README.txt (used by the doctest) and the test data folder shapefiles/ are not installed by setup.py.

If you aren't in the pyshp folder, running shapefile.test() from the command line doesn't end well:
```
$ python3 -c 'import shapefile; shapefile.test()'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/shapefile.py", line 1186, in test
    doctest.testfile("README.txt", verbose=1)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/doctest.py", line 2039, in testfile
    encoding or "utf-8")
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/doctest.py", line 221, in _load_testfile
    file_contents = package.__loader__.get_data(filename)
  File "<frozen importlib._bootstrap>", line 1623, in get_data
FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: '/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/README.txt'
```

Perhaps the `test` function should be renamed to `_test`, to show that it is a private function.

A little more documentation (README?) on testing pyshp for the people contributing to the project.

issue_title: Negation case-sensitivity
issue_body:
Hello,

Today I randomly tried to evaluate the following sentence: 'Not bad at all'. To my surprise, the compound was negative so I was trying to find out why would it.

After a quick read of the source code, I understood that Vader only handles lowercase negation.
Is there any solution to this?

A first instect is to .lower the whole text, but that would make things terrible, I'll be losing some key-features of Vader like  GOOD vs good etc..

I'm thinking (as a last resort) to split the words, check NEGATE contains their lowercase form and re-write the sentence with the negations in lowercase, but I'm hopping for a better solution.

Thank you.

issue_title: Missing dependencies (requests)
issue_body:
Hello!
When attempting to run `vaderSentiment` after installing it in my venv with the suggested `pip install vaderSentiment`, I got the following error:

```

  File "rate.py", line 1, in <module>

Seems that `requests` is an implicit(?) dependency.
I looked for a `requirements.txt` but not sure that's how it works with packages up on PyPI.

My `pip freeze` suggests that a `requirements.txt` for `vaderSentiment` could look like this:

```
certifi==2018.4.16

Let me know if this is something I can help with - would gladly do so :)
